flashcards.front: What is the three-step process of Divide and Conquer paradigm?
flashcards.back:
1) DIVIDE: Break problem into smaller subproblems of same type
2) CONQUER: Recursively solve subproblems
3) COMBINE: Merge solutions to get original problem's solution

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of Merge Sort?
flashcards.back: O(n log n) in all cases (best, average, worst). The divide step takes O(1), conquer takes 2T(n/2), and merge takes O(n).

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of Quick Sort in worst case?
flashcards.back: O(n²) occurs when pivot is always the smallest or largest element (array already sorted). Average case is O(n log n).

flashcards.front: What is Strassen's Matrix Multiplication time complexity?
flashcards.back: O(n^2.807) which is better than naïve O(n³). Uses 7 multiplications instead of 8 for 2×2 matrices.

flashcards.front: How does Binary Search achieve O(log n) complexity?
flashcards.back: By repeatedly dividing search space in half. At each step, compare middle element with target and eliminate half of remaining elements.

flashcards.front: What is the Closest Pair of Points algorithm complexity using Divide and Conquer?
flashcards.back: O(n log n). Sort points by x-coordinate, recursively find closest pairs in left/right halves, then combine using strip of width 2d.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of FFT (Fast Fourier Transform)?
flashcards.back: O(n log n) using divide and conquer. Breaks DFT of size n into two DFTs of size n/2 using even and odd indices.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between external and internal recursion in Divide and Conquer?
flashcards.back: Internal: All subproblems solved within same function. External: Subproblems passed to different recursive calls (like Merge Sort's merge step outside recursion).

flashcards.front: What is the Median of Medians algorithm used for?
flashcards.back: To find median or kth element in O(n) time worst-case. Used as pivot selection in Quick Sort to guarantee O(n log n).
Algorithms that perform optimally regardless of cache size, using divide and conquer to achieve good spatial and temporal locality automatically.

flashcards.front: What is the Master Theorem for Divide and Conquer?
flashcards.back: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n), where a≥1, b>1. Compares n^(log_b a) with f(n) to determine complexity. Cases: equal, smaller, or larger than f(n).

flashcards.front: Why is Quick Sort preferred over Merge Sort for arrays?
flashcards.back: Quick Sort has better cache performance due to in-place partitioning. Merge Sort needs O(n) extra space. However, Merge Sort is stable.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the combining step in Divide and Conquer?
flashcards.back: To merge solutions of subproblems into solution of original problem. This step often determines overall efficiency (e.g., merge in Merge Sort is O(n)).

flashcards.front: What is the recurrence relation for Merge Sort?
flashcards.back: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n), with base case T(1) = O(1). Solves to O(n log n) by Master Theorem.

flashcards.front: What is the divide step in Quick Sort called and what does it do?
flashcards.back: Partitioning. It rearranges elements so all elements less than pivot are on left, all greater are on right. Pivot goes to its final position.
